glossary.definition: A chunk of text, like a syllable or part of a word, that the AI reads instead of whole words or letters.
A program that chops your normal English text into tokens so the AI can process them.
A free tool you can use on your own computer to count exactly how many tokens your text will use before you send it to the AI.

hook.scenario: You paste a 1,000-word essay into GPT-4o and hit the context limit — but your essay is only 1,000 words. The limit is 128,000 tokens. What went wrong?
hook.question: Why is 'token' not the same as 'word', and why does this distinction control your API bill?

mental_model.intuition: A token is a chunk of text — roughly a word, a syllable, or a punctuation mark — that the model reads as one atom.
mental_model.description: LLMs never read characters or words: they read tokens. A tokenizer splits text into tokens before feeding it to the model. English common words are usually one token. Rarer words, code, and non-English text split into multiple tokens. On average ~4 characters = 1 token in English. Every price on the OpenAI pricing page, every context-window limit, and every rate limit is expressed in tokens, not words.

illustration.alt: The phrase 'unbelievable pricing' split into four sub-word token tiles, counted as 4 tokens.

sections.body: A tokenizer is the first thing that runs when you call an API. It converts raw text into a sequence of integer IDs, each mapping to one sub-word piece. The model never sees letters — it sees the ID sequence. On the other side, when the model outputs, a de-tokenizer converts IDs back to text.
sections.recap: Text → token IDs → model → token IDs → text. Characters never touch the model.

sections.body: Why sub-words, not whole words? Whole-word vocabularies need millions of entries for all words, plurals, conjugations, and proper nouns. Sub-word tokenisers (OpenAI uses tiktoken with BPE) use a fixed ~100k-entry vocabulary and split unknowns into known pieces. 'unbelievable' → ['un', 'believ', 'able']. This handles all languages and arbitrary code without an infinite dictionary.
sections.recap: Sub-word BPE: fixed vocabulary, unknown words split into known pieces.

sections.body: Token count drives three things: cost (you pay per token in and out), latency (more tokens = more time), and context limits (the model has a hard cap on total tokens it can process in one call — input + output combined). The 128k context window on gpt-4o means 128,000 tokens, not words. In English prose, 1k tokens ≈ 750 words. In Python code, tokens are denser — a function can cost 2–3× more tokens than you expect.
sections.recap: Tokens = the billing and limits unit. 1k English tokens ≈ 750 words.

sections.body: You can inspect tokenization before making an API call. The tiktoken library mirrors OpenAI's tokenizer exactly — call it locally, pay nothing, see the split and the count. This is the standard way to guard against accidental overshooting the context limit before you send the request.
sections.recap: Use tiktoken locally to count tokens before calling the API — free, instant.

sections.body: The surprising insight: non-English text and code often use far more tokens per 'word' than English prose. A Chinese character may be 1–2 tokens; a rare technical term in English can be 3–4. This means the same content costs differently depending on language and domain — a fact that trips up teams who budget based on word count.
sections.recap: Tokens, not words, control cost, context limits, and rate limits — and they vary dramatically by language and domain.

code_snippets.title: Count tokens before making an API call
code_snippets.code:
import tiktoken

enc = tiktoken.encoding_for_model('gpt-4o')
text = 'unbelievable pricing for the new plan'
tokens = enc.encode(text)

print(len(tokens))           # 7
print(enc.decode(tokens))    # round-trips back to the original text
code_snippets.explanation: tiktoken mirrors OpenAI's tokenizer exactly with no network call. Use this to count tokens before you send — it prevents silent context-overflow bugs and lets you budget before you pay.

code_snippets.title: Count tokens in a messages[] list (the real billing unit)
code_snippets.code:
import tiktoken

def count_messages_tokens(messages: list[dict], model='gpt-4o') -> int:
    enc = tiktoken.encoding_for_model(model)
    total = 0
    for msg in messages:
        # 4 tokens overhead per message (role + delimiters)
        total += 4 + len(enc.encode(msg.get('content', '')))
    total += 2  # reply priming tokens
    return total

msgs = [
    {'role': 'system', 'content': 'You are a helpful assistant.'},
    {'role': 'user',   'content': 'Explain RAG in one sentence.'},
]
print(count_messages_tokens(msgs))  # ~27
code_snippets.explanation: The API bills the full messages[] array, not just the user message. Each message carries ~4 overhead tokens for its role tags. Accumulate this across a long conversation and the cost grows faster than you expect.

common_mistakes.title: Equating tokens with words in cost estimates
common_mistakes.explanation: Budgeting 'it's only 500 words' without counting tokens. In English ~1.3 tokens/word; in code or other languages it can be 2–4×. Always run tiktoken on your actual content.

common_mistakes.title: Forgetting the context limit is tokens in + tokens out
common_mistakes.explanation: A 128k context window counts both the input you send AND the output the model generates. If your prompt is 120k tokens, you have only 8k left for the response — the model will truncate or error.

recall_questions.q: What is a token, and roughly how many tokens is 1,000 English words?
recall_questions.answer: A token is a sub-word piece (roughly a word or syllable). 1,000 English words is roughly 1,300 tokens.

recall_questions.q: Why do LLMs use sub-word tokenization instead of character-level or whole-word?
recall_questions.answer: Sub-word BPE gives a fixed, manageable vocabulary that handles rare words, new terms, and multiple languages without an infinite dictionary.

recall_questions.q: Which library lets you count tokens locally before calling the OpenAI API?
recall_questions.answer: tiktoken — it mirrors OpenAI's exact tokenizer and runs locally with no API cost.

oa_questions.question: A team estimates their RAG prompt will be '3,000 words' and is surprised when the API throws a context-length error. What are two likely causes?
oa_questions.answer: 1) Word count ≠ token count — 3,000 words is ~4,000 tokens, not 3,000. 2) The context limit counts input + output tokens combined; if the output budget is also being consumed, the effective input limit is smaller. Fix: count with tiktoken, not words.
oa_questions.approach: Convert words→tokens (~1.3×) and flag the input+output combined limit. These two explain nearly every 'why did I hit the limit?' surprise.

oa_questions.question: You're building a multilingual support bot that handles English and Thai. Your English content costs $0.50 per 1M tokens at your expected volume. A colleague expects Thai to cost the same. Why might it cost significantly more?
oa_questions.answer: Non-Latin scripts often tokenize less efficiently — a Thai character may be 2–3 tokens where an English word is 1–1.3 tokens. The same information in Thai can cost 2–3× more in tokens. Always benchmark actual token counts per language on your real content.
oa_questions.approach: Anchor on the sub-word vocabulary point: BPE was trained primarily on English text, so non-English scripts get split into more, smaller pieces.
